title: SEGMENT 10 — Only 20,000 genes, and 50% of the genome is repeats: the great surprises

lines.text: SEGMENT 10 — Only 20,000 genes, and 50% of the genome is repeats: the great surprises
Welcome back.
We are now into the aftermath — what we found when we actually read the sequence, and what those findings have meant for biology.
The single most surprising result of the Human Genome Project, the one that has reshaped how we think about genetics, was the gene count.
Going into the 1990s, the standard textbook estimate for the number of protein-coding genes in the human genome was around one hundred thousand.
Some estimates went higher — one hundred twenty thousand, one hundred fifty thousand.

The reasoning was straightforward and seemed obvious: humans are complex, we have hundreds of cell types, an immune system that generates astronomical antibody diversity, a brain with eighty-six billion neurons, the most sophisticated cognition of any organism on earth.
Surely we needed a lot of genes to encode all that.
There was even a famous betting pool, organized at a Cold Spring Harbor meeting in 2000, called GeneSweep.
Scientists put money down on what the final gene count would be.
The estimates ranged from twenty-six thousand on the low end to one hundred fifty thousand on the high end.
The median bet was around sixty thousand.
When the 2001 draft papers were analyzed, the estimate came in at around thirty-two thousand genes.
When the 2003 finished sequence was annotated, the estimate dropped to around twenty-five thousand.
As gene annotation has been refined over the subsequent two decades, the current consensus estimate is approximately nineteen to twenty thousand protein-coding genes.
Twenty thousand.
For context, the nematode worm Caenorhabditis elegans — a microscopic creature with exactly 959 somatic cells and a nervous system of 302 neurons — has about twenty thousand protein-coding genes.
The fruit fly Drosophila melanogaster has about fourteen thousand.
A rice plant has about thirty-five thousand.
The unicellular protozoan Paramecium has about forty thousand.
By gene count, you are about the same as a worm and substantially less complicated than a grain of rice.
This was a genuine shock to the field.
It violated a deep intuition that complexity at the organism level should correspond to complexity at the gene level.
How do you reconcile this?
Where does human complexity actually come from, if not from more genes?
The answer is what genomics has been working out for the last twenty years, and it has three main components.
First: alternative splicing.
A single gene can produce multiple different protein products by splicing its exons together in different combinations.
The average human gene produces about four distinct splice isoforms, and some genes produce dozens.
So twenty thousand genes can produce on the order of a hundred thousand or more distinct proteins.
Second: regulation.

The human genome contains an enormous amount of regulatory sequence — promoters, enhancers, silencers, insulators, microRNA binding sites — that controls when, where, and how much each gene is expressed.
The regulatory complexity is vastly greater in humans than in worms or flies, even when the protein-coding gene count is similar.
Complexity lives in the wiring diagram, not in the parts list.
Third: networks.
Genes do not act alone; they act in networks of interactions, feedback loops, and pathways.
The combinatorial complexity of a network grows much faster than the number of nodes in the network.
Twenty thousand genes can produce far more biological complexity through their interactions than the number twenty thousand might suggest.
Here is the analogy.
Twenty thousand genes is the parts list of a piano — eighty-eight keys, a few thousand internal mechanical parts.
The number of melodies you can play on that piano is essentially infinite.
Complexity is not in the parts; it is in the patterns of activation.
The second great surprise of the Human Genome Project was the amount of the genome that is not protein-coding.
Of the three billion bases, the protein-coding sequences — the actual exons of the actual genes — total roughly sixty million bases.
That is about two percent of the genome.
Two percent.
The other ninety-eight percent breaks down roughly as follows.
About half the genome — fifty percent — is repetitive DNA derived from transposable elements: LINEs, SINEs, Alu repeats, endogenous retroviruses.
These are sequences that at some point in our evolutionary past were active transposons, jumping around the genome and copying themselves, and most of them are now defective relics.
Another twenty-five percent is intergenic sequence of various kinds.
About twenty percent is intronic — within genes but not protein-coding.
A few percent is regulatory and structural sequence.
And the rest is various other categories.
The Alu repeats alone account for about ten percent of the entire genome.
Ten percent of your DNA is roughly one million copies of a single three-hundred-base-pair repeat element derived from a primate-specific transposon.
This was, at the time, often described as "junk DNA," a term coined in 1972 by Susumu Ohno to describe non-coding sequence that appeared to have no function.
The HGP findings sharpened the question: is ninety-eight percent of your genome actually junk, or does it do something we have not figured out yet?
That question is what ENCODE, the next segment, was designed to answer.
And the ENCODE answer turned out to be controversial — productively controversial.
